videos.title: how much does Georgia really cost? │ 2025 full cost of living
videos.description:
"Real Estate in Georgia" - https://korter.ge/en/ 

hello :) this video is a detailed breakdown of the cost of living in Tbilisi, Georgia. from apartments to groceries, eating out, transportation, and fun, I’m breaking down the real costs of daily life here in the capital city. whether you’re thinking of moving, traveling long-term, or just curious, this video gives you the full picture of living in Tbilisi on a budget or more comfortably.

0:00-0:30 intro
0:31-2:05 rent
2:06-2:41 sim cards
2:42-3:06 wifi
3:07-3:11 water
3:12-5:25 groceries
5:26-5:44 cafes
5:45-6:39 restaurants
6:40-7:44 transport
7:45-8:06 fun stuff
8:07-8:30 health insurance
8:31-9:13 wrap it up
videos.transcript: If you're voluntarily spending more than like a weekend in Tbilisi, Georgia, maybe you're moving here, maybe you booked a one-way ticket after a questionable life decision, maybe you just didn't book a flight home. This is for you. Tbilisi has one of the lowest cost of living in Europe and West Asia. And if you're from one of 98 countries, you can stay here for a whole year. No visa, no awkward embassy interview where they ask you why you came to Georgia and you panic and start saying things like >> Uh, mountains and wine. >> Though that would be a really good answer. So, you're here and probably you're going to be looking for four walls and a roof. If you want to book before coming here, you're probably going to look to book on one of two websites, which are booking.com and Airbnb. So, on Airbnb and booking.com, the average price of an apartment per month, including utilities and Wi-Fi, ranges from between $250 to $700 per month. The vast majority of hosts have a 30 to 50% discount if you book for at least 1 month, so that's what we always do. Of course, it can be a bit more, a lot more, and a bit less depends on which neighborhood you're staying in, how big the place is, the quality of it, etc. I'm splitting an apartment with my boyfriend and we each pay $280 per month. Four main areas that foreigners seem to stay in, which are Sololaki. That's where I am now. It's very cute and historic, also close to the Old Town. Then there is Vera, which is kind of between historic and really modern, [music] international. There is Vake. That is definitely the most bougie one. It has really leafy, beautiful streets, a lot of international restaurants, gyms, co-working spaces, things like that. Lastly, there is Saburtalo. That's where a lot of university students stay. That will be the most affordable option. As I'm staying in Tbilisi for 2 months, I cannot speak for talking to local real estate agents because I don't have experience with that. However, that is of course an option. If you are chronically online, like me, you're going to want data. Unfortunately, getting a SIM card here in Georgia is stupidly easy. You can get one, a physical SIM card, at the airport for free [music] with a few gigabytes of data. You can also get a physical SIM card at one of many kiosks in the Old Town or downtown. The price [music] ranges from between 5 to 10 dollars for about 5 to 15 gigabytes of data, which is pretty [music] good. Getting an eSIM is even easier. Here are some websites that I recommend for getting an eSIM in Georgia. I got one from Selfie with unlimited data for 779, which is great. If you're a digital nomad, you'll definitely worrying about the internet speed. We found that from contacting many, many, many different hosts on booking.com and Airbnb, the average internet speed is between [music] 40 to 70 Mbps. However, when we asked about it, most of them even responded, >> Oh, we can double the speed for you. >> Like for free or for a small fee per month. Georgian hosts are just like that. Yeah, water. It is drinkable. Everybody drinks it. I haven't gotten sick, haven't gotten a rash. Don't spend your money on bottled water. We found four main supermarkets in Tbilisi, >> which are Spar, Ori Nabiji, Carrefour, and Goodwill. >> The first one, Spar. This is probably the most common one. You can see it like every other block. It has a good number of products, but the range of brands isn't that big. And also, it tends to be pretty expensive. >> Then there's Ori Nabiji. We go to this one almost every single day to get, you know, a little something. >> Great option. Also very cheap, like 50% sometimes cheaper than Spar. >> Then there is Carrefour. Carrefour is like the you know, mini Costco. It's like a mini Costco of Georgia. You can find everything at Carrefour, but there aren't that many Carrefours. You have to like, you know, make a little bit of a trek to get there sometimes. So, we go here maybe once a week or once every other week. Then there is Goodwill, and Goodwill is like the Whole Foods of Georgia. It is very high-quality, but it's also more expensive. If you're looking for organic products or imported stuff, you can most likely find it at a Goodwill. Now, on grocery prices. Of course, items that are produced in Georgia or neighboring countries are going to be a lot cheaper than imported stuff. I'll just go through the average prices. So, let's take a walk through the supermarket. 1 L of regular milk, $1.86. One loaf of regular sandwich bread, $0.74. 1 kg of rice, $2.97. 1 kg of fresh chicken breast, $6.68. [music] 1 kg of beef, $11.13. 1 kg of watermelon, which I've been eating a heck lot of because it costs $0.37 per kilogram. 1 kg of peaches, $1.48. I forgot to film the eggs, but a carton of 12 eggs will be $2.23. 1 kg of imported cheese like from the Netherlands or France, $7.42. But if you get to Georgian cheese, which is delicious and there are a lot of different types of because Georgia produces a lot a lot of cheese, around $3.71. 500 ml soft drink will be around $0.74. [music] A local beer from the supermarket, $1.52. Bottle imported beer, around $2.23. You can find a mid-range wine bottle for around $7. These are just the grocery prices. If you go to bazaars or local produce markets, you can often find the prices for 20 to 30% lower. If you sometimes like to come out of your cave and see other people, there are cafes everywhere. Coffee culture here is great. And you can find a cup of coffee for between $1 to $4. $1 will be more like a Turkish coffee or something of an espresso. And then $4 will be more like an oat milk latte, worth it. Maybe. If we're looking at street food, you can find khachapuri, which is a bread with like cheese inside, from between around $0.37 to $1.80. A Georgian style hot dog will be between $0.74 and $1.11. If you want to eat some good Georgian food for cheap, I recommend going to one of those places with the old style buildings that is kind of in a hidden alley. The quality of food is always, almost always, quite good and it is really affordable as well. So, the price of this kind of place or maybe a cafe or like canteen style place can usually be between $3 to $6 per meal. If you are looking at an inexpensive restaurant, but maybe in a main touristy area, it can be between $8 to $12. A mid-range restaurant, probably between $16 to $26. And a fancier restaurant will be maybe around $50. And the price of international restaurants can be quite expensive, actually. For example, meat donor with rice, $10.95. Now, a topic that is very near and dear to my heart, public transportation. If you're in Tbilisi and you don't want to log 50,000 steps per day, you're going to want to use the bus or subway. And fortunately, it is very affordable. It is one Georgian lari or around $0.37 per tap. And you can tap with your credit card or debit card or you can get a Metromoney card, which will allow your, you know, one Georgian lari fare to last 90 minutes with unlimited transfers. And also, the quality of the buses and subways, like, it is pretty good. It is really clean. It is usually on time. Traffic hour, nah. But, yeah, that is just kind of a universal thing. Tbilisi is actually a really walkable city, except for it does have a lot of cobblestone streets and uneven sidewalks and stray dogs. Um and for those reasons, it's not a super bike-friendly city unless you are an experienced cyclist. I do see some bike lanes, especially in the Vake and Vera districts, which also tend to be a lot flatter. But, in most of the neighborhoods, I wouldn't recommend biking here unless you are experienced. I haven't tried it. Um yeah. Random costs, if you like nice things sometimes. An eyebrow wax will be between $5 to $10. A massage between $20 to $40. A sulfur bath experience, which is a very Georgian thing, between 15 to $30. And the price of a gym membership is oddly a bit expensive here, between 40 to $60 per month. I can just hike up the mountain for free. Health insurance, it really varies between expats. If you're staying here long-term, you might be eligible for Georgian health insurance, which is like $250 per year, or you know, from $250 per year, which is much lower than international health insurance. It can literally be between $30 to $400 per month, but it really depends on your nationality and things like this. Do your own research on that. So, what does it cost in total to live in Tbilisi per month? [music] It's hard to say because everybody's lifestyle is insanely different. For me, in my first month, I spent $681. And [music] um I haven't moved here. Like this is just based on my observations and experiences from traveling or staying here for an extended period of time. We aren't [music] living luxuriously, but we have gotten a nice apartment in a lovely area. We can eat well and enjoy good coffee, and still have [music] money left over for some adventures. But if you're curious to try Tbilisi out, well, it is architecturally stunning. [music] It is very sunny, has a lot of good Wi-Fi spots, and it's safer than almost any other country in Western Europe [music] or North America. I would recommend

safetyNotes: As I'm staying in Tbilisi for 2 months, I cannot speak for talking to local real estate agents because I don't have experience with that
Don't spend your money on bottled water
If you're in Tbilisi and you don't want to log 50,000 steps per day, you're going to want to use the bus or subway
recommendations: Here are some websites that I recommend for getting an eSIM in Georgia
And then $4 will be more like an oat milk latte, worth it
If you want to eat some good Georgian food for cheap, I recommend going to one of those places with the old style buildings that is kind of in a hidden alley
But, in most of the neighborhoods, I wouldn't recommend biking here unless you are experienced

videos.title: What Not to Miss in the Georgian Capital Tbilisi
videos.description:
Georgia, a country located the Caucasus region in Eastern Europe and West Asia, might not have been in contention as your next travel destination - but this video will change your mind! Let us show you around Georgia’s fascinating capital Tbilisi - from the beautiful old town, to wellness spas and bunkers from the Cold War era. Last but not least, we introduce you to the delicious local food.

00:00 Intro
00:29 Where Tbililsi is located
00:36 Cable car to viewpoint, Narikala fortress, Mother Georgian
01:26 Old town
02:54 Leaning clock tower
03:05 Sioni Cathedral
03:18 Gulo's Thermal Spa
04:10 Georgian food
04:52 Nutsubidze Plato I Micro-District
05:17 Explore old bunkers from Soviet times
06:44 Nightlife in Fabrika

CREDITS:
Report: Anja Koch
Camera: Cem Madmo Springer
Editing: Frederik Willmann
Supervising editor: Christina Deicke
Thumbnail with picture alliance
-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Subscribe to DW Travel
youtube.com/dwtravel

Would you like to find out more about DW Travel? 
▸Facebook: facebook.com/dw.travel
▸Instagram: instagram.com/dw_travel

DW Travel is showing you the captivating world of travel. Come along with us to regions and cities in Germany, throughout Europe and to far-away destinations.

#georgia #tbilisi #tbilisitravel #traveling #dwtravel
videos.transcript: Today I'm exploring what could be one of the most underrated capitals – Tbilisi, the beating heart of Georgia. The city combines 19th century wooden buildings, Soviet high rises, amazing food and beautiful views. I'll visit one of the legendary sulfur baths, taste Georgian dumplings and descent to a secret underground area of the city. We'll also check out some nightlife. Tbilisi is located in the center of Georgia and is the largest city in the country by far. I'm starting my day at Tbilisi's cable car, a pretty efficient way to reach our first highlight of the town. Join me for some amazing views! The cable car takes me over the river and the old town, offering fantastic 360° views. The ride only takes a few minutes. And even better views await me. Pretty, isn't it? At the top are the ruins of the 4th century Narikala Fortress and the 20 meters high "Mother of Georgia" monument. She looks out over the city from above, holding a bowl of wine in one hand for friends and a sword on the other for enemies. A leisurely stroll downhill will take you to the old town with its charming alleyways and famous balconies. Most houses have those wooden carved balconies and are often built around courtyards. Let's find out what this has to do with typical Georgian lifestyle. Baia Dzagnidze is a travel blogger who grew up in Tbilisi, so she knows her way around here well. She shows me one of the most famous courtyards in the old town. The balconies here don't only look good, they are the heart of social life. It's very easy to gossip between each other or also know what your neighbor is doing or what kind of guests they are getting. Or what their daily routine is pretty much every day. Another thing I notice in Tbilisi is that there are often renovated houses next to those that are in need of work. This is what I call a "Soviet mentality". Because when we became part of the Soviet Union, everything started to be cared for you. The government was doing everything for the people. They were giving flats for free and there were services for free, as well. So a lot of people even today think that the government should do it. We continue to the Clock Tower, one of Tbilisi's landmarks. But there's something unusual about it— it's leaning. A few steps later, I reach the 1500-year-old Sioni Church. Religion is very important in Georgia and the Orthodox Church has a lot of influence. I think it's about time to relax. The area surrounding me is the perfect place for that. Why? Let's find out. This is a thermal bath with sulphur water from a hot spring. Georgians love to bathe in this water, which is around 35°C. Smells a bit like rotten eggs, but it's apparently good for your health. The sulfur water is said to be good for the skin and respiratory system and helps promote blood circulation. And also, of course, what we really need, the neurological system to calm us down and to really feel the ease and relaxation. The rooms are rented out individually. Interestingly, these springs once gave the city its name. Tbilisi translates to "warm place". The history of these baths spans centuries. I'm hungry now and I really want to try some Georgian food. Georgian's love to order lots of different dishes like beetroot and spinach spreads as starters, salad and the national dish Khinkali, which are basically soup dumplings. There's a special way to eat them. You grab them by the tail. Turn them around, bite a little hole in it, and sip out the soup. Delicious. Georgia was part of the Soviet Union until 1991. This also left its mark on the architecture. Take these brutalist style buildings for example, with their exposed concrete and geometric shape. The bridge that connects these residential buildings is also open to tourists. Traces of the Soviet Union can also be found underground. Tinatin Goguadze loves exploring the city's underground system of former bunkers, tunnels and torture chambers. This was the control panel room. She shows me the largest command bunker in the country. This is where the KGB, the Soviet Secret Service, was based until the fall of the Iron Curtain. With the control panel for communication with the outside and tubes for measuring radiation in case of a nuclear attack, you quickly feel transported back to the Cold War period. It's very interesting to imagine how it was before. And that is very interesting for me. That's the main reason why I like to look for this kind of locations. Tinatin Goguadze now also guides tourists through these underground areas. She first had to clear trash from the bunkers, but she felt that should be done anyway. And then we started to renovate and clean these kind of places. Because in case of something happens, we think we must be ready. These places are very useful and people can survive in some way, like if something happens. Do you think you might need those again? I think you should always be ready. I end my day in Tbilisi here: in Fabrika. This former sewing factory is a thriving cultural center with a hostel, artist studios, cafés and bars where people can enjoy the nightlife. It's time to try some Georgian wine. Cheers. Or, as Georgians would say, Gaumarjos!

safetyNotes: The balconies here don't only look good, they are the heart of social life

Tourist Scams in Georgia & How to Avoid Them.
As in any other city around the world in Tbilisi also you may come across some common tourist scams and usually tourists fall when they are not aware of them.
Watch the full video to see tips on how to avoid being scammed.

Check for more info: 
https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCIsqke5Tw8c0f7lEGvUhg_A
videos.transcript: Hi there! Tourist scams can happen in many cities of the world and Tbilisi is NOT exception! As a local guide I hear some of the tourist scams and in today's episode I'm going to share the most common tourist scams in Tbilisi, Georgia, that way you can avoid the scams and enjoy with your holidays much better! Now let me share this scams to you: First of all, after arrival at Tbilisi airport you will be met by taxi drivers who will suggest use their service to take you to the Old City in some cases, at the end of the ride they may ask you some ridiculous prices for their service somebody was overcharged with 300,- Georgian Lori from airport till the city! Keep in mind that the maximum tariff what you should expect from airport till the city ranges from 30 to 60 Georgian Lari In Tbilisi we don't have taxi meters and some foreigners can be overcharged by random taxi drivers Pay attention to the numbers and commas at the money exchange offices, especially at the airport and in Old City because sometimes these numbers and commas can be very "tricky" and you can find the money exchange offices just next to each other and with quite much difference in the rates! Instead, I advice to o convert money in less touristic parts of the city for more information about money exchange recommendations please check our Channel: Money Exchange video that is specially dedicated to this topic The next scam what I'm going to share is pretty common in many cities of the world and not only in Tbilisi! Avoid suspicious bars, with "too friendly" hosts or pretty girls who are promo agents and try to persuade you to enter at their space Here they will try to overcharge you for some unclear services or products! usually this type of bars even does not have any reviews or location pointed as a Google Map.. My recommendation is to be careful when somebody is trying or advertising "too much" their product, because good things does not always need so much advertising! Sometimes you can come across really good, authentic place without any reviews at all! So the reviews does not always look as a "guarantee" if the place is good or not Whenever you decide to purchase some product or service in the street or Market, always ask the price in advance Avoid places where the prices are not clear /fixed, Instead, try to find the place where all the prices are visible and marked in advance that will prevent you from future confusion whenever purchasing your products Bonus tip: Old Tbilisi has become very touristic area and here you can come across too many agents who will suggest you some services: it will be photos with trained animals or street performances or rides... if you are not interested, I recommend just to continue your walk, but if you are interested always make sure to double check and ask the price of the service in advance! this will prevent from future confusion and misunderstanding At Last I want to say that Tbilisi is very safe destination to travel and enjoy with your time but it does not mean that you totally need to ignore your personal safety during the travel... Information is Power and traveling in a Smart Way saves you from possible unpleasant surprises and makes your trip smooth positive and memorable Well, I hope you will find this video before your arrival to Tbilisi, Georgia and you find it very helpful during your stay here! Make sure to subscribe our channel to stay updated with other cool videos. See you next time bye!

videos.title: Four Best Neighbourhoods to Live in Tbilisi
videos.description:
Join Our Email List and be the First to Hear about Breaking News and Exciting Offers
https://nomadcapitalist.com/email

Get on the waiting list and join us for the next Nomad Capitalist Live:
www.nomadcapitalist.com/live/

Tbilisi is a small city by most measures. Yet there is a huge degree of difference between the different districts and neighborhoods. 

Drawing boundaries around the different neighborhoods in Tbilisi is not an easy task. While the 10 administrative districts (raions) are fairly clear, within those 10 there are dozens of ‘micro-districts’ that sometimes overlap and intersect, plus informal historic neighborhoods that people refer to by different names. Then there are nicknames on top of that, often based on the nearest avenue or metro station. Tbilisi is very safe and you can still find real estate at affordable prices. 

In this video, Lika Tsintsabadze, Business Lawer, will share four of the best neighborhoods to live in Tbilisi. 

00:00 Start
0:11 Living in Tbilisi
1:03 The Safest Places in Tbilisi
2:53 Real Estate in Tbilisi
3:25 Rustaveli Avenu In Tbilisi
5:25 Living in Vake
7:44 Investing in Tbilisi

Andrew Henderson and the Nomad Capitalist team are the world's most sought-after experts on legal offshore tax strategies, investment immigration, and global citizenship. We work exclusively with seven- and eight-figure entrepreneurs and investors who want to "go where they're treated best".

Work with Mr. Henderson: https://nomadcapitalist.com/apply/

Andrew has started offshore companies, opened dozens of offshore bank accounts, obtained multiple second passports, and purchased real estate on four continents. He has spent the last 12 years studying and personally implementing the Nomad Capitalist lifestyle.

Our growing team of researchers, strategies, and implementers add to our ever-growing knowledge base of the best options available. In addition, we've spent years studying the behavior of hundreds of clients in order to help people get the results they want faster and with less effort. 

About Andrew: https://nomadcapitalist.com/about/
Our Website: http://www.nomadcapitalist.com
Subscribe: https://www.youtube.com/subscription_center?add_user=nomadcapitalist
Buy Andrew's Book: https://nomadcapitalist.com/book/

DISCLAIMER: The information in this video should not be considered tax, financial, investment, or any kind of professional advice. Only a professional diagnosis of your specific situation can determine which strategies are appropriate for your needs. Nomad Capitalist can and does not provide advice unless/until engaged by you.
